cards.definition: A testable but not-yet-confirmed proposed explanation for a phenomenon. Once rigorously confirmed, it can become a theory.
A confirmed explanation for WHY a phenomenon occurs. It survives repeated testing — not 'just a guess' in the colloquial sense.
A generalized pattern observed in nature, described mathematically (e.g., F=ma). Describes WHAT happens, not WHY.
Karl Popper's test: a real scientific claim must be testable in a way that could prove it WRONG. Science advances by disproving.
When the available data fits multiple competing hypotheses. More or different data is needed to distinguish them.
The default 'nothing happening' assumption — no effect, just chance. Statistical tests try to reject it.
Comparing experimental and control groups while changing only ONE variable at a time to isolate cause.
Studying patterns without manipulating variables. Reveals correlations, not causation.
The angle of Polaris above the horizon equals your LATITUDE in the northern hemisphere.
15 degrees per hour (360°/24h). Each time zone spans roughly 15° of longitude.
Earth's rotation relative to the STARS: 23h 56m. Shorter than the solar day because Earth also moves along its orbit.
Earth's rotation relative to the SUN: 24 hours. Longer than sidereal because Earth orbits the Sun while spinning.
The Sun's apparent yearly path across the sky — also the plane of Earth's orbit. The zodiac constellations lie along it.
Earth's 23.5° tilt is what causes seasons — sunlight strikes more directly in summer, obliquely in winter. Distance from Sun barely changes.
We are NOT the center of the universe — nothing about our location or viewpoint is privileged.
N = R* × fp × ne × fl × fi × fc × L. Estimates the number of communicating civilizations in our galaxy.
Star formation rate in the Milky Way (~5–10 Sun-like stars per year). First multiplier.
Fraction of stars that host planets. Kepler showed this is close to 1 — most stars have planets.
Number of habitable-zone planets PER planetary system (~1 for Sun-like stars).
fl: fraction where life arises. fi: fraction where intelligence evolves. fc: fraction with detectable communication. Deeply uncertain.
Lifetime of a detectable civilization. The MOST uncertain term — ranges from decades to billions of years.
If intelligent life is common, where is everybody? Naive math says we should see signs already.
An object at rest stays at rest, an object in motion stays in motion, unless acted on by a force. Inertia.
F = ma. Net force equals mass times acceleration. Doubling thrust doubles acceleration; halving mass also doubles it.
Every action has an equal and opposite reaction. Why rockets work — exhaust pushed down, rocket pushed up.
1) Orbits are ellipses. 2) Planets sweep equal areas in equal times. 3) Period² ∝ semi-major axis³.
Energy is neither created nor destroyed, only transformed between forms. Total stays constant in a closed system.
Gravity, electromagnetism, strong nuclear force, weak nuclear force.
Weakest fundamental force, but infinite range. Dominates at large scales (planets, galaxies). g = 9.8 m/s² on Earth.
Holds protons and neutrons together in the nucleus. Strongest force, but extremely short range.
Distance between successive crests of a wave. Longer wavelength = lower frequency / lower energy.
Number of wave cycles per second. Measured in hertz (Hz). Higher frequency = higher energy.
≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s in vacuum. The cosmic speed limit. c = λ × f.
E = hf. Photon energy is proportional to frequency, with h (Planck's constant) as the link.
Electrons can only occupy specific quantized energy levels, explaining why atoms emit/absorb only specific wavelengths.
Bright lines at specific wavelengths produced by hot, low-density gas. A unique fingerprint of each element.
Dark lines on a continuous spectrum where cool gas absorbs specific wavelengths. Reveals composition of stars.
Peak emission wavelength is inversely proportional to temperature. Hotter objects peak at shorter wavelengths (bluer).
Radio → Microwave → IR → Visible → UV → X-ray → Gamma. Higher frequency = shorter wavelength = higher energy.
Uses LENSES to bend light. Limited by lens weight; rare for large modern instruments.
Uses MIRRORS to focus light. Most large modern telescopes are reflectors.
Deformable mirror flexes in real time to cancel atmospheric turbulence. Sharpens ground-based images.
Slow corrections to mirror shape against gravity, thermal flexing, wind. Used on Rubin and other survey scopes.
Resolution ∝ wavelength / aperture. Longer wavelengths or smaller mirrors = blurrier images. Fundamental physics.
Mirror size — controls how faint an object you can see. More area = more photons captured per second.
Ability to separate two close sources. Image sharpness.
Ability to resolve photon energies/wavelengths into fine bins. Critical for composition analysis.
Ability to detect changes in brightness over time. Critical for transits, pulsars, GRBs.
Counting total photons (brightness). Used for transits and variable stars.
Splitting light by wavelength to reveal composition, motion, and physical conditions.
X-rays skim heavy-metal mirrors at <1° angles, like skipping stones. Used by Chandra; needed because X-rays don't reflect head-on.
Combining signals from multiple antennas with precise timing. Resolution = baseline (max distance between antennas), but sensitivity = sum of individual areas.
Space-based X-ray telescope. ACIS detector logs every photon's X, Y, time, and energy.
James Webb Space Telescope — 6.5m segmented gold mirror, primarily IR. ~7× Hubble's collecting area.
2.4m visible/UV/near-IR space telescope. Above the atmosphere, not limited by seeing.
Detects gamma rays via electron-positron pair production tracks. Above atmosphere because gamma rays are blocked.
4-km laser interferometer detecting GRAVITATIONAL WAVES (spacetime stretches), not EM radiation.
Atacama Large Millimeter Array (Chile). Observes mm/sub-mm radio — cold gas, dust, protoplanetary disks.
8.4m primary mirror, huge field of view. Surveys the entire southern sky every few nights for LSST.
Murchison Widefield Array (Australia). Phased-array radio telescope — points electronically, no moving parts.
Sun-like, yellow, ~10 Gyr lifetime, moderate abundance. Best-studied for life because it's our home star.
Orange dwarf — cooler/dimmer than Sun, tens of Gyr lifetime, ~3× more abundant than G. The 'Goldilocks' star type.
Smallest/coolest, MOST abundant (~75%), very long-lived, but prone to violent flares with high X-ray/UV. Tight habitable zones.
Range of distances from a star where liquid water can exist on a planet's surface. Goldilocks zone.
Close-in planets always show the same face to their star. Risk for habitable-zone planets around M-dwarfs.
M-dwarf with 7 Earth-sized transiting planets. JWST found TRAPPIST-1d has no detectable atmosphere — bad sign for M-dwarf habitability.
Detect exoplanets by periodic dimming as they cross in front of their star. Used by Kepler and TESS.
Detect exoplanets by Doppler-shift wobble of the host star as the planet tugs it.
Six elements essential for life: Carbon, Hydrogen, Nitrogen, Oxygen, Phosphorus, Sulfur. Carbon is central — forms 4 stable bonds.
Carbon forms 4 stable covalent bonds, enabling complex molecules (proteins, DNA, etc.) impossible with other elements.
Universal solvent, high heat capacity, expands when frozen (ice floats), polar molecule. Why it's essential for life.
Life's building blocks formed ON Earth (e.g., Miller-Urey-style chemistry in early atmosphere/oceans).
Life's building blocks DELIVERED from space via asteroids/comets. Bennu samples support this — contain amino acids.
Period ~4.1–3.8 Gya when Earth was pummeled by asteroids/comets. May have delivered water and organics.
Carbonaceous asteroid sampled by OSIRIS-REx. Returned material with amino acids — supports exogenous delivery hypothesis.
Carbonaceous — most common type, dark, water/organic-rich. Bennu is C-type.
Silicaceous — stony, rocky composition. Second most common.
Metallic — high iron/nickel content. Believed to be cores of differentiated bodies.
1953: simulated early-Earth atmosphere + electric sparks produced amino acids. Showed life's building blocks form from inorganic precursors.
Chemical (CHNOPS-based reactions), Biological (reproduces, evolves, metabolizes), Astronomical (biosignatures detectable from afar).
Anything (molecule, isotope ratio, gas mix) that indicates the presence of life. Astronomers look for these in atmospheres.
Differential survival/reproduction based on heritable traits. Acts on existing variation.
Random changes in DNA. The ONLY mechanism that introduces NEW genetic variation.
Random changes in allele frequencies, especially in small populations. Acts on existing variation.
Movement of alleles between populations via migration. Acts on existing variation.
Rapid loss of large fractions of species. Five known in Earth's history; reset evolutionary trajectories.
Organism thriving in extreme environments — heat, cold, acid, radiation, salt, pressure. Expands where we look for life.
Forward force produced by expelling mass (exhaust) backward. Newton's 3rd law in action.
Resistive aerodynamic force opposing motion through atmosphere. Largest near sea level; negligible in vacuum.
Aerodynamic force perpendicular to direction of motion. Helps winged vehicles; minor for vertical-launch rockets.
Downward force from gravity = mass × g. The force a rocket must overcome at liftoff.
Rotational force — causes spin/tilt around an axis. Rockets must counter unwanted torque to fly straight (gimbaled engines, fins).
a = (T - mg) / m. Thrust must exceed weight for liftoff. Doubling thrust OR halving mass both double acceleration.
Efficiency of a rocket engine — thrust per unit propellant flow rate. Higher Isp = more bang per kg of fuel.
Change in velocity needed for a maneuver. The 'currency' of orbital mechanics — every orbit/escape requires a delta-v budget.
Δv = Isp × g × ln(m_initial / m_final). Limits how much delta-v you get from a given fuel mass.
Minimum speed to escape a body's gravity without further propulsion. Earth: ~11.2 km/s. Moon: ~2.4 km/s.
Speed needed to maintain orbit at a given altitude. Low Earth orbit: ~7.8 km/s.
Dropping spent fuel tanks/engines to reduce mass mid-flight. Critical for reaching orbit — single-stage-to-orbit is impractical with chemical fuel.
Burns fuel + oxidizer for high thrust but limited Isp. The only tech currently capable of Earth-to-orbit.
Accelerates ions electrically — very high Isp but tiny thrust. Great for long deep-space missions, not for launch.
SpaceX reusable rockets. ~$1,500/kg to LEO — order of magnitude cheaper than legacy expendable launchers.
Recovering and re-flying boosters. Key SpaceX innovation driving cost reduction.
Manufacturing-Aware Hardware Development. Iterative, build-test-learn approach. Good for novel/uncertain products; bad for high-stakes one-shot designs.
Babson's Entrepreneurial Thought & Action framework. Act-learn-build-act. MAHD is its hardware analogue.
Spacecraft passes a target without stopping. Cheapest, fastest reconnaissance — but limited observation time.
Enters orbit around target for sustained observation. More expensive (delta-v to brake) but rich data.
Touches down on surface for in-situ measurements. Adds entry/descent/landing complexity.
Mobile lander. Lets you sample multiple sites. Perseverance, Curiosity on Mars.
Bring material BACK to Earth for advanced analysis. OSIRIS-REx (Bennu), Mars Sample Return (planned).
Measuring a target from a distance — cameras, spectrometers, radar. No physical contact.
Physical contact with target — sample collection, drilling, atmospheric probes.
Preventing biological contamination of other worlds (forward) or Earth (backward). Drives sterilization and end-of-mission disposal.
Deliberately crashed into Jupiter (2003) to prevent any contamination of potentially habitable Europa.
Scientific goals drive instrument choice; cost/mass constraints limit how many you can fly. Always a tradeoff.
Past liquid water, current thin CO2 atmosphere. Perseverance hunts biosignatures in Jezero Crater. Targeted by sample return.
Jupiter's icy moon with subsurface ocean under ~10–25 km ice shell. Heated by tidal flexing. Europa Clipper launching in 2024–25 era.
Saturn's small icy moon. Cassini found cryovolcanic plumes from a subsurface ocean — possible target for Orbilander.
Saturn's largest moon. Thick N2 atmosphere, liquid methane lakes, complex organics. Dragonfly will fly there in the 2030s.
NASA-ESA mission (1997–2017). Orbited Saturn; Huygens probe landed on Titan. Discovered Enceladus plumes and Titan's lakes.
NASA orbiter to study Europa's habitability. Suite includes ice-penetrating radar, magnetometer, mass spectrometer, cameras.
NASA rotorcraft mission to fly across Titan's surface in the 2030s. Will hop to multiple sites for prebiotic chemistry studies.
NASA Mars rover (2021–). Drilling and caching samples at Jezero Crater for future Mars Sample Return.
Sampled asteroid Bennu (2020), returned to Earth (2023). Found amino acids — supports exogenous delivery.
Proposed Enceladus mission concept — orbits, then lands. Would directly sample plume material on the surface.
Hierarchy of distance methods. Each rung calibrates the next: parallax → Cepheids → Type Ia supernovae → Hubble's law.
Apparent shift of nearby stars against distant background as Earth orbits. Works only for nearby stars (~few hundred pc with Gaia).
Pulsating star whose pulsation PERIOD reveals its true LUMINOSITY. Compare to apparent brightness → distance. Reaches nearby galaxies.
Standard-candle explosion of a white dwarf. Always near-identical peak brightness — used for far-galaxy distances.
An object of known intrinsic luminosity. Compare to apparent brightness → infer distance.
v = H₀ × d. Galaxies recede at speeds proportional to distance — evidence for expanding universe.
Stretching of light to longer wavelengths from receding sources. The further away, the higher the redshift.
Universe began ~13.8 Gya from a hot, dense state and has been expanding/cooling since. CMB is its leftover glow.
Cosmic Microwave Background — uniform 2.7K microwave glow from when the universe became transparent ~380,000 years after Big Bang.
Einstein 1905. Speed of light is constant for all observers; space and time depend on observer's frame.
Einstein 1915. Mass and energy curve spacetime; gravity is geometry, not a force. Confirmed by 1919 eclipse (light bending).
Moving clocks tick slower from a stationary observer's frame. At 0.95c, ~3× slowdown.
Moving objects appear shorter in direction of motion from a stationary frame. Pairs with time dilation.
Traveling twin returns younger because they accelerated/turned around — broke symmetry. Resolved by general relativity.
Nothing with mass can reach c. Energy needed approaches infinity. Sets the floor for interstellar travel times.
Alcubierre's idea — contract space ahead, expand behind. Doesn't violate c locally but requires exotic negative-energy matter.
How close a measurement is to the TRUE value. Different from precision.
How close repeated measurements are to EACH OTHER. You can be precise but inaccurate (consistent bias).
Range within which the true value likely falls. Reported as ±. Limited by instruments, repetition, technique.
T = 2π√(L/g). Period depends only on length and gravity. Solving for L: L = g(T/2π)².
Measures fraction of incident light reflected at each wavelength. Used by ALTA II to identify minerals on Mars-analog soils.
Reflectance spectrometer used in Lab 5. Shines 11 LEDs at sample, measures reflected intensity to ID minerals.
Atmospheric gases (CO2, H2O, CH4) absorb outgoing IR, warming the surface. Lab 3 models it with bottles + temperature probes.
Continuous (hot dense object), Emission (hot low-density gas), Absorption (cool gas in front of continuous source). Lab 2.
